title: CI/CD Supply-Chain Compromise (Poisoned Dependency)
description: Response playbook for a software supply-chain incident where a malicious or compromised third-party package is pulled into the build, executing attacker code in the CI pipeline and exfiltrating build secrets. Triggered by a dependency-confusion/typosquat alert, an unexpected outbound connection from a build runner, or a disclosed compromise of an upstream package.
author: Cognis Digital

steps.name: Pin and vet dependencies
steps.description: Use lockfiles with hashes, an internal package mirror/allowlist, and scoped registries to reduce dependency-confusion and typosquat risk.

steps.name: Constrain and monitor build runners
steps.description: Give CI runners least-privilege, short-lived credentials and restrict their egress so an injected step cannot freely call out or reach production.

steps.name: Identify the malicious package and build
steps.description: Pinpoint which dependency, version, and pipeline run introduced the malicious code.
steps.detection: Build logs and lockfile diffs for an unexpected new/changed dependency; CI runner egress logs showing connections to an unfamiliar host during install or build.

steps.name: Scope what secrets and artifacts were exposed
steps.description: Determine which CI secrets and signing keys the compromised step could read and whether any published artifact is tainted.
steps.detection: Audit which environment variables/secrets were available to the build, and check artifact registries for anything published from the affected runs.

steps.name: Freeze the pipeline and quarantine artifacts
steps.response: Pause affected pipelines, remove or pin away from the malicious package version, and quarantine any artifacts built from compromised runs to stop downstream spread.

steps.name: Revoke exposed secrets
steps.response: Rotate every credential, token, and signing key the compromised build could access, treating all of them as exposed regardless of confirmation.

steps.name: Remove the tainted code path
steps.response: Replace the poisoned dependency with a vetted version from a trusted source, rebuild from clean inputs, and verify the lockfile hashes.

steps.name: Re-release clean artifacts
steps.response: Rebuild and re-sign artifacts with rotated keys, publish clean versions, and notify any downstream consumers of tainted releases.

steps.name: Harden the build supply chain
steps.description: Enforce an internal mirror/allowlist, require hash-pinned lockfiles, add SBOM and provenance checks, and tighten runner egress and secret scoping.
